title: What Counsel Has The Hooded Moon
content:
What counsel has the hooded moon
    Put in thy heart, my shyly sweet,
    Of Love in ancient plenilune,
    Glory and stars beneath his feet,
    A sage that is but kith and kin
    With the comedian Capuchin?

    Believe me rather that am wise
    In disregard of the divine,
    A glory kindles in those eyes
    Trembles to starlight. Mine, O Mine!
    No more be tears in moon or mist
    For thee, sweet sentimentalist.
meaning:
"What Counsel Has The Hooded Moon" is a quintessential example of James Joyce's signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
